title: TomaccoTimer: From Static UI to Generative UI
content:
A Pomodoro timer is the dumbest possible productivity app. Twenty-five minutes of work, five minutes of break, a sound at the end. There's nothing to design.

So I built one. Three hundred lines of Swift, AppKit, a floating panel that sits above every other window. Done.

Then I gave it a name that felt proportionate to the idea: **TomaccoTimer**. A pomodoro timer cross-bred with something more addictive.

![The static timer panel](/pomodoro-genui/01-timer-panel.png)

Then I tried to *use* it. And it turns out the timer is the easy part. The hard part is staying in the chair for the next twenty-five minutes.

## The First Gamification

I added a layer on top: every minute I focus is tuition tax I won't have to pay next year. €3000 of tuition divided across ~500 study hours = ~€0.10 per minute of work. A big green number ticks up. A goal bar walks toward €3000. A level label goes from 🌱 Freshman to 👑 Master as pomodoros stack.

![Money-saved panel](/pomodoro-genui/02-earnings-panel.png)

It works. Embarrassingly well, actually. Watching a number go up while you do something hard is *real*. I shipped this in an evening and immediately got more focused work done than the previous week.

But here's where the design breaks.

The `€/hr` field exists because the savings metric needs a rate. The level label exists because the pomodoro count is a metric. The goal bar exists because the savings metric has a target. **Every motivation idea drags its own UI behind it.**

Add "30-day streak" and you need a day counter and a flame icon. Add "study partner waiting" and you need an avatar and a status. Add "trees planted per pomodoro" and you need a tree icon and a CO₂ counter.

A static gamification layer is one cell of a Cartesian product, and the product has thousands of cells, because motivation is *deeply personal*.

## Money Doesn't Motivate Everyone

The tuition-tax framing works for me because of my specific situation: Italian university tuition, plus the implicit threat of a retake. It would not motivate:

- The student who's bored, not broke
- The person whose family is paying anyway
- The hobbyist learning on weekends
- The PhD candidate on a stipend

For them, the motivating story is different. A streak. A friend. A leaderboard. A Tamagotchi. A tree being planted. A parent's voice memo. Some of those aren't even quantifiable in euros.

So you can either:

1. Ship 20 toggleable gamification modes, which becomes a combinatorial nightmare.
2. Let the UI itself be **generated** from the user's stated motivation.

## The Pivot: Generative UI

The shape of the bet:

> The pomodoro core stays static. The motivation layer above it is generated by a local AI from a sentence the user types.

The flow:

1. First launch: a text field. *"What would actually keep you in the chair?"*
2. The user types: *"I want to save €3000 in tuition I'd pay if I fail."* Or: *"I want to keep a 30-day streak with my friend Marco."* Or: *"Plant a tree every 4 pomodoros."*
3. An on-device LLM emits a constrained **Gamification Spec**.
4. The Swift app validates the spec against a known vocabulary: counter, progress bar, level, badge, input field, formula.
5. The renderer builds the actual panel widgets and wires them to pomodoro events.
6. The *spec*, not generated code, is persisted. Same spec, same UI, every launch.

The pomodoro engine doesn't know or care what's drawn above it. It emits events. The motivation layer subscribes.

![The motivation wizard](/pomodoro-genui/04-wizard.png)

![The combined working panels](/pomodoro-genui/03-both-panels.png)

## Why Swift Is Actually a Good Fit

Two things that don't usually get mentioned together:

**SwiftUI is declarative.** A view is a function of state. So "render this spec as a view tree" is a natural fold: walk the spec, return the corresponding `View`. No imperative plumbing, no manual frames, no layout math. The renderer is small.

**Apple shipped local AI as a system framework.** macOS 26 Tahoe introduced **Foundation Models**, the system's on-device language model with a Swift-native API. No API keys, no cloud roundtrips, no marginal cost per generation.

```swift
import FoundationModels

@Generable
struct GamificationSpec {
    @Guide(description: "Metrics tracked over time")
    let metrics: [Metric]

    @Guide(description: "User-editable inputs")
    let inputs: [Input]

    @Guide(description: "Visual widgets to display")
    let widgets: [Widget]

    @Guide(description: "Pomodoro events that update metrics")
    let subscriptions: [Subscription]
}

let session = LanguageModelSession(instructions: systemPrompt)
let response = try await session.respond(
    to: userMotivation,
    generating: GamificationSpec.self
)
```

That `@Generable` macro is the important shift. The model is constrained to emit a value of the right Swift type, so the output is already shaped for the app instead of being a blob of almost-JSON that needs post-processing.

This is the same general idea behind [`flutter_local_ai`](https://pub.dev/packages/flutter_local_ai): a unified API over Apple's Foundation Models on iOS/macOS and ML Kit Generative AI on Android. Same shift, different surface: let local models do what cloud models used to.

## The Architecture

```text
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  USER: "I want to save €3000 in tuition tax."               │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                            │
                            ▼
            ┌────────────────────────────────┐
            │   On-device FoundationModels   │
            │   → GamificationSpec (typed)   │
            └────────────────────────────────┘
                            │
                            ▼
            ┌────────────────────────────────┐
            │   Validator (Swift)            │
            │   - widget vocabulary check    │
            │   - formula language check     │
            │   - persisted in UserDefaults  │
            └────────────────────────────────┘
                            │
                            ▼
            ┌────────────────────────────────┐
            │   Renderer (SwiftUI)           │
            │   spec → View tree             │
            │   metrics → UserDefaults keys  │
            └────────────────────────────────┘
                            │
                            ▼
            ┌────────────────────────────────┐
            │   Pomodoro Engine (static)     │
            │   onTick / onComplete / onSkip │
            │   → drives metric updates      │
            └────────────────────────────────┘
```

The spec is the contract. Everything else is interchangeable.

A second user types *"I want a 30-day streak with my friend Marco."* The model produces a different spec: `streak`, `today_done`, `last_day_iso` metrics, a `🔥 N day streak` counter, a `Marco can be proud today` badge. Same renderer. Same pomodoro engine. Completely different motivation layer. **No code change.**

## What I'm Watching Out For

**Determinism.** Generate once, then freeze. LLMs are stochastic; users hate it when their app rearranges itself overnight.

**Validation, not trust.** The spec only references widgets and formulas the renderer already knows about. No `eval`, no arbitrary code, no remote fetches.

**Latency.** Generation is a setup-time event. The runtime hot path is spec-on-disk to SwiftUI views. Zero network calls.

**Graceful degradation.** If the model emits something the validator rejects, fall back to a curated template: money saved, streak, plant a tree. Generative as a layer on top of good defaults, not a replacement.

## The Real Point

It's not about AI rendering UIs. That's the technique.

It's about the **boundary between the fixed shape of an app and the personalized layer on top.** Productivity tools have spent decades trying to find one motivation story that works for everyone, and they end up with feature toggles, themes, and gamification packs the user has to assemble themselves.

Generative UI flips the labor. The user tells the machine what they want to feel; the machine builds the surface that produces that feeling. The app becomes a runtime for the user's own psychology, not a fixed metaphor they have to adopt.

A pomodoro is a tiny canvas. But the pattern scales: habit trackers, language apps, journals, fitness, savings. Anywhere the *core loop is universal but the motivation is private*, generative UI is the natural shape.

The interesting code in TomaccoTimer isn't the timer. It's the layer above the timer that a year ago would have been hard-coded by the developer and is now better expressed as data generated from the user's own motivation.

Now it does.
excerpt: TomaccoTimer started as a tiny macOS Pomodoro app, then turned into a generative-UI experiment: keep the timer static, let local Foundation Models build the motivation layer from whatever actually pushes the user to focus.
